steps.title: Identify the key evidence about conventional fabrication
steps.content:
The passage states that conventional ETL fabrication loses up to 80% of solution, explicitly increasing the cost of manufacturing at scale. This is the central problem the researchers are trying to solve with spray coating. Look for the choice that captures this limitation of conventional methods for large-volume production.
  • Choice A — It is less suitable for manufacturing large volumes of planar p-i-n perovskite solar cells than an alternative fabrication method may be.: Directly addresses the scale problem — conventional fabrication is less suitable for large-volume manufacturing than spray coating. ✓
  • Choice B — It is more expensive when manufacturing at scale than are processes for fabricating ETLs used in other perovskite solar cell architectures.: Compares conventional to other perovskite architectures, not to spray coating specifically. The passage does not support a cross-architecture cost comparison. ✗
  • Choice C — It typically entails a greater loss of nanoparticle solution than do other established approaches for ETL fabrication.: Calls other approaches "established," mischaracterizing spray coating, which the passage presents as a newer alternative, not an established standard. ✗
  • Choice D — It is somewhat imprecise and therefore limits the potential effectiveness of p-i-n planar perovskite solar cells at capturing and storing solar power.: Introduces unsupported claims about cell effectiveness; the passage focuses on manufacturing cost, not effectiveness at capturing solar power. ✗

steps.title: Confirm the match and eliminate
steps.content: Only the matching choice accurately captures the passage’s claim: conventional fabrication is inferior to spray coating for large-scale manufacturing. The other choices either compare the wrong things or introduce ideas not found in the passage. A is correct.
